description: NEW FILE docs/api/current/methodology.json added in commit 6e06efb 'Add methodology-first audit layer and claim taxonomy'. File explicitly scopes OpenTimestamps: 'OTS proves that a hashed file snapshot existed by a given time; it does not by itself prove that every claim inside that file was an isolated pre-release forecast.' Introduces five-class claim taxonomy (prospective_confirmed, retrospective_structural, supportive_nonunique, pending_contested, open_refinement) and instructs auditors to 'verify the numerical threshold or directional wording predates the confirming data pull.' This is RHETORICAL acknowledgement of the exact OTS wrong-side critique documented on our Timestamp Error tab, WITHOUT a corresponding structural fix (no predictions-only file + separate OTS anchor, no monitor.py change separating prediction registration from observation recording, no new cryptographic mechanism).
before: methodology.json did not exist. OTS anchoring presented as blanket proof of prospectivity. Registry counts presented as single score.
after: methodology.json exists and explicitly narrows OTS to snapshot-existence only; registry counts explicitly labelled 'bookkeeping, not a substitute for claim-by-claim audit'; five-class claim taxonomy introduced; anti-strawman rules added.
canary_notes: SOFT-TRIGGER. The poller.md canary definition says it triggers when the author 'separates prediction documents from observation documents and timestamps them independently' — that structural fix has NOT happened. BUT per poller.md guidance to cross-reference trigger_signals against 'changes to how predictions are registered or validated — not just text in commit messages', this methodology.json qualifies. The author has adopted our exact critique language (snapshot-vs-claim-level prospectivity, 'directional wording predates the confirming data pull') while leaving the underlying OTS anchoring untouched. Analyst must decide: (a) is this a genuine canary fix requiring our Timestamp Error tab to evolve; or (b) is it rhetorical evasion — patching the narrative, not the code — which is an even stronger data point about reactive patching?
